title: The Truth About Netflix Buying Warner Bros. Plus: Snubs and Surprises of the Golden Globe Nominations and Jafar Panahi on ‘It Was Just an Accident.’
format: Sean Fennessey and Amanda Dobbins open on the $82.7 billion news that Netflix's bid for Warner Bros. was accepted, and what it does to theatrical windows, physical media, and the studios left standing (Paramount's hostile counter-bid landed an hour before they recorded). Then the Golden Globe nominations, snub by snub. Sean closes with an interview with Jafar Panahi about 'It Was Just an Accident,' recorded before news broke of Panahi's latest one-year prison sentence.

segments.heading: Netflix buys Warner Bros.
segments.note: the $82.7B deal, and Paramount's hostile counter-bid

films.title: Frankenstein
films.note: Cited as the pattern they fear: a short theatrical run, then straight to the service, and the casual viewer learns to wait.

films.title: Wake Up Dead Man
films.note: The latest Knives Out, doing the same quick-to-Netflix routine.

films.title: Jay Kelly
films.note: Maybe not even getting the theatrical moment, per Amanda.

films.title: Sinners
films.note: Ryan Coogler's 25-year ownership stake used as the example of a deal a Netflix-owned WB might not greenlight.

films.title: Barbie
films.note: Sean's read on Greta Gerwig: she keeps mining her youth, and that's now steering her toward IP and the big Netflix-scale release.

films.title: Lady Bird
films.note: Amanda's Gerwig ranking: No. 1, ahead of Little Women and Barbie.

films.title: Little Women
films.note: No. 2 on Amanda's Gerwig list, by a smidgen.

films.title: Oppenheimer
films.note: Sarandos once claimed it and Barbie would've been just as big straight to streaming, which Amanda calls a crazy thing to say out loud.

films.title: Train Dreams
films.note: The counter-case: a small film Netflix acquired and put in front of everyone, that would've had a tiny windowed release as a Sony Classics title.

films.title: Triple Frontier
films.note: Named as one of the actually-good Netflix originals. The list is shorter than the creative freedom would suggest.

films.title: Zootopia 2
films.note: $900 million in two weekends: the money is still there in theatrical when the concept is big enough.

films.title: Top Gun: Maverick
films.note: Skydance's one clear win; they were right to sit on it through the pandemic.

films.title: Furiosa: A Mad Max Saga
films.note: Sean's post-box-office essay from May 2024, when 'just keep going to movies' was still an easy message to send.

films.title: KPop Demon Hunters
films.note: Sony gave it to Netflix; used as shorthand for the smaller studios leaning on streaming-first deals.

films.title: A New Leaf
films.note: Elaine May's film, newly on 4K via Cinematograph/Vinegar Syndrome and licensed from Paramount. Sean's worry is whether Netflix would ever license out a WB catalog title that way.

films.title: Bring It On
films.note: Amanda's 4K, made by Shout Factory, one of the labels now routing distribution through the WB/Universal-owned SDS.

films.title: F1
films.note: $11.99 to own on 4K forever, cheaper than a ticket, and the case for physical media as preservation insurance.

films.title: One Battle After Another
films.note: Nine nominations, in every major category it was eligible for. Running in comedy, which they think is backwards. Regina Hall the only arguable snub.

films.title: Sentimental Value
films.note: The second-most-nominated film with four acting nods and two for Joachim Trier, including director. A surprise, given it had felt like it was waning.

films.title: Hamnet
films.note: Six nominations (Chloé Zhao, Jessie Buckley, Paul Mescal, Max Richter's score), pretty much exactly what it needed.

films.title: Sinners
films.note: Running in drama against Hamnet despite its musical elements; not a single supporting nod, which Sean calls the biggest oversight.

films.title: Nouvelle Vague
films.note: Richard Linklater's second Best Musical or Comedy nominee, the pick that threw Sean for a loop, and at Jay Kelly's expense.

films.title: Blue Moon
films.note: Linklater's other one, with Ethan Hawke in for Best Actor: 'wonderful.'

films.title: Jay Kelly
films.note: Shut out of Best Musical or Comedy, but Clooney and Sandler both got acting nods; no Billy Crudup.

films.title: Wicked for Good
films.note: Left out of Best Musical or Comedy entirely and relegated to the cinematic-and-box-office category. Big news, since it's sinking at the box office.

films.title: Bugonia
films.note: Three nominations, in comedy, with Jesse Plemons and Emma Stone: funny, but debatable how funny versus painful.

films.title: A House of Dynamite
films.note: Completely shut out.

films.title: Wake Up Dead Man
films.note: Also completely shut out.

films.title: Avatar: Fire and Ash
films.note: Relegated to the cinematic-and-box-office-achievement category: cinematic first, then box office.

films.title: F1
films.note: Sean's beloved, also in the blockbuster category.

films.title: KPop Demon Hunters
films.note: In the box-office category, plus two of its songs nominated; won Best Animated Feature at the New York Film Critics Circle. 'Justice for Soda Pop.'

films.title: Frankenstein
films.note: Five nominations, including Oscar Isaac and Best Director for Guillermo del Toro, but not screenplay. One of two Netflix drama spots, over Jay Kelly and Train Dreams.

films.title: Train Dreams
films.note: No Best Drama nod, though Joel Edgerton got in. Lost a Netflix slot to Nouvelle Vague and Frankenstein.

films.title: Marty Supreme
films.note: Snubbed for its score (which Sean calls maybe the score of the year) and for Josh Safdie in director, the Ben Affleck-hosted screening notwithstanding. Sean saw it a second time and it was better.

films.title: No Other Choice
films.note: Lee Byung-hun and the film in for Best Musical or Comedy, a satirical, violent comedy Sean just put on his top five.

films.title: The Smashing Machine
films.note: Emily Blunt in supporting actress: 'some Globes got a Globe.'

films.title: Hedda
films.note: Tessa Thompson in for Best Actress, on Amazon Prime now; an interesting adaptation that doesn't quite get there. Nina Hoss was good, and snubbed.

films.title: Sorry, Baby
films.note: Eva Victor nominated for Best Actress, never would've predicted it. Still a real Best Original Screenplay contender at the Oscars.

films.title: Sirât
films.note: Nominated for its electronic rave score, the most outlandish pick to Sean, who has it on Blu-ray but hasn't seen it. More like Challengers than Marty Supreme.

films.title: Challengers
films.note: The comparison point for the Sirât score, and the reminder the Globes skipped it last year too.

films.title: Song Sung Blue
films.note: The Kate Hudson karaoke movie neither host has seen yet.

films.title: Anaconda
films.note: On Amanda's to-see list, the reason she can't get to Song Sung Blue this week.

segments.heading: ‘It Was Just an Accident’
segments.note: the hosts' take before the Panahi interview

films.title: It Was Just an Accident
films.note: Panahi's Palme d'Or winner, shot largely in secret: a mechanic thinks a man who drives in after an accident may be his former torturer, and a Hitchcockian road comedy accretes around the question. Amanda went in cold and loved it, stressed the whole time; both flag a hospital set piece for its nausea. Sean's pick for this year's 'Anatomy of a Fall.'

films.title: This Is Not a Film
films.note: Made under house arrest, the experimental end of Panahi's work that this film moves away from.

films.title: Taxi
films.note: The one where he mounts a camera in a taxi, cited as the kind of formally playful film that 'It Was Just an Accident' deliberately isn't.

films.title: If I Had Legs I'd Kick You
films.note: Amanda's other most-nauseous-in-a-theater movie of the year, and a companion to the hospital sequence here.

films.title: Anatomy of a Fall
films.note: The template: the international thriller that becomes the awards conversation's pop favorite. Sean thinks this is that film this year.

segments.heading: Jafar Panahi on ‘It Was Just an Accident’
segments.note: recorded before news of his latest one-year sentence

films.title: It Was Just an Accident
films.note: Panahi (through interpreter Shaida Dayani) says he doesn't see it as a thriller: his wife watches those, he runs away. Revenge and forgiveness are only surface; the real question is whether the cycle of violence keeps repeating. He cast for physical presence first, drew the roof-and-credit-card-machine scene from prison and from a kid with a card reader at a red light, and wanted the humor to run until the last twenty minutes so the ending would land. He knows only that his next film is about war.

films.title: Bashu, the Little Stranger
films.note: Panahi's answer to the closing 'last great thing you've seen': Bahram Beyzai's newly restored 1989 film, which he notes is also about war.

TV: The Sex Lives of College Girls

Wake Up Dead Man.title: Wake Up Dead Man: A Knives Out Mystery

If I Had Legs I'd Kick You.title: If I Had Legs I'd Kick You
